summary: The founding Chairman of the People's Republic of China (declared October 1, 1949), whose aphorisms were collected in the Little Red Book and whose portrait still hangs over Tiananmen Square. Mao Zedong rose to leadership of the Chinese Communist Party during the Long March at the Zunyi Conference, developed his own variant of Marxism-Leninism in essays like "On Contradiction," and launched the Hundred Flowers Campaign, the catastrophic Great Leap Forward (which caused a massive famine), and the Cultural Revolution, during which he was nearly the target of a coup ("Project 571") by his designated successor Lin Biao. His death in 1976 and the subsequent arrest of the Gang of Four, led by his widow Jiang Qing, ended the Cultural Revolution. He remains a fixture of Chinese and world literature, opera (John Adams's Nixon in China), and visual art (Andy Warhol's silkscreens).

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Mao Zedong is most often identified as the founder of a namesake variant of Marxism-Leninism whose aphorisms were collected in the Little Red Book, and as the first Chairman of the People's Republic of China, whose founding he declared on October 1, 1949, and whose portrait still hangs over Tiananmen Square. He is also frequently identified as the leader of China during the Cultural Revolution.

works.name: Rise to Power: The Long March & the Second United Front
works.description: Mao rose to leadership of the Chinese Communist Party by taking power from Bo Gu and Otto Braun (leaders of the "returned student faction") at the Zunyi Conference during the Long March. In Yan'an, he narrated his autobiography to the American journalist Edgar Snow, who published it as Red Star Over China, improving his public image (he referred to the atomic bomb as a "paper tiger"). He allied with his rival Chiang Kai-shek against Japan following the Xi'an Incident, forming the Second United Front, and around this time developed his theories of protracted guerrilla warfare while living in a cave.

works.name: Political Philosophy: "On Contradiction" & Other Writings
works.description: As a theorist, Mao is best known for the essay "On Contradiction," in which he compared opposing classes to a bomb — "a single entity in which opposites coexist" until an explosion occurs — and argued that contradictions are universal. He originated the phrases "political power grows out of the barrel of a gun" and "women hold up half the sky," divided the world into three categories of nations in his "Three Worlds Theory," delivered the Talks at the Yan'an Forum on Literature and Art, and wrote an essay praising Norman Bethune, a man he had met only once, for his "spirit of absolute selflessness."

works.name: The Little Red Book
works.description: Mao's quotations were assembled and published as the "Little Red Book," compiled by his eventual designated successor Lin Biao, who delivered a speech praising Mao as a genius. Widely distributed during the Cultural Revolution, it includes sections on "Democracy in the Three Main Fields" and "Criticism and Self-Criticism" (urging avoidance of subjectivism and the elimination of beatings in the army) and on the "Mass Line," which claims that "the people alone are the motive force in world history." A portrait originally included in the book became the basis for Andy Warhol's famous series of silkscreen paintings of Mao.

works.name: The Hundred Flowers Campaign
works.description: In 1956, Mao briefly encouraged Chinese citizens to voice their true opinions of the government, comparing "the flourishing of arts and the progress of science" to "a hundred flowers blooming." The movement was short-lived, and its speech, "On the Correct Handling of the Contradictions Among the People," was followed by a crackdown on the very critics it had drawn out.

clues.clue: Encouraged citizens to voice their true opinions of the government in 1956, comparing artistic and scientific flourishing to "a hundred flowers blooming"
examples: Early during his rule, this leader compared "the flourishing of arts and the progress of science" to "one hundred flowers [blooming]."
The aforementioned speech was given by this man, the Chairman of the Communist Party and center of a cult of personality during his rule of China.

works.name: The Great Leap Forward & Famine
works.description: Mao's Great Leap Forward included a campaign against the "Four Pests" (such as sparrows) and forced peasants toward metal production, contributing to a famine remembered as the "three years of natural disasters." The overreporting of crops planted through "deep plowing" — connected by the book Tombstone to his "General Line" concept, one of the Three Red Banners — accompanied accusations of mass cannibalism; propaganda even staged a photo of children standing on a hidden bench atop a thickly planted wheat field. The era's abuses are documented in books including The Corpse Walker, the pseudonymous Scarlet Memorial by T.P. Sym, and Frank Dikotter's The Tragedy of Liberation.

works.name: The Cultural Revolution & Cult of Personality
works.description: During the Cultural Revolution, Mao gifted Pakistani mangoes (originally a gift from Foreign Minister Mian Arshad Hussain) to workers after they suppressed student revolts; unwilling to eat them, recipients preserved the mangoes in formaldehyde as cult symbols. He called himself the "black hand" suppressing rival student factions and told dissenters to "Bombard the Headquarters" amid corruption accusations aired on "big-character posters," later titling his own poster the same. He nearly fell victim to "Project 571," a coup plot by his designated successor Lin Biao, who fled and died in a plane crash in Mongolia after the plot was uncovered; he also summoned the leaders of a local government formed by the "January Storm" (modeled on the Paris Commune) and forced them to normalize their rule, and feuded with the president of the Chinese Academy of Sciences over a reconciliatory poem. He also claimed to have swum 15 kilometers in 65 minutes in 1966 — mocked by Judy-Joy Davies as "the greatest swimmer of all time" — after having swum across the Yangtze River in 1956 and again in 1966. His death in 1976 and the subsequent arrest of the Gang of Four, led by his wife Jiang Qing, brought the Cultural Revolution to an end.

works.name: Wives of Mao Zedong
works.description: Mao's abandonment of his wife He Zizhen and subsequent marriage to Jiang Qing was criticized in Ding Ling's essay "Thoughts on March 8" and later depicted in Anchee Min's novel Becoming Madame Mao. His first wife was active in the Cultural Book Institute and was executed by a warlord after refusing to recant, screaming a preference for decapitation. He Zizhen, whose legitimacy as his wife was later denied, was a skilled guerrilla fighter educated at Moscow's East University; interviews with Edgar Snow and Clare Hollingworth are the only major sources on her before she was supplanted by Jiang Qing, a former Lu Xun Academy teacher who went on to design several "model operas," feuded with Liu Shaoqi and Zhou Enlai, and was ultimately removed from power via a secret meeting orchestrated by Li Xiannian; after Mao's death, Jiang Qing led the Gang of Four.

works.name: Cultural Depictions: Literature, Opera & Visual Art
works.description: Mao's name is rhymed with the line "you ain't gonna make it with anyone anyhow" in the Beatles' "Revolution 1," and he appears as a recurring narrator (alongside Pablo Picasso) in Campbell McGrath's poetry collection XX. Don DeLillo's novel Mao II, titled for an Andy Warhol silkscreen of him, opens with a mass Unification Church wedding ceremony and closes with a photographer's account of Ayatollah Khomeini's funeral and a Beirut wedding procession. Mao himself wrote the poem "Reply to Li Shuyi," playing on the literal meanings of two surnames. A giant portrait of him unnerves the narrator in Perhat Tursun's Uyghur-language novel The Backstreets; his surname recurs in Madeleine Thien's Do Not Say We Have Nothing and Anchee Min's Becoming Madame Mao, and the Misty Poets rejected the literary classicism he championed. In John Adams's opera Nixon in China, Mao is visited by Richard Nixon, emerges from a hidden door in a giant self-portrait to sing "I am no one," and is the subject of an orchestral foxtrot intermezzo. He has been painted waving from a boat in Strive Forward in Wind and Tides, reimagined visiting Venice by Erro Gudmundur, depicted with blue eyeliner and rouge in a 14-foot portrait at the Art Institute of Chicago, rendered as three iconic jackets in the sculpture Legacy Mantle, and portrayed in a widely reproduced (900-million-times-printed) contrapposto image as a young man traveling to Anyuan.

works.name: Global Influence & Foreign Relations
works.description: Mao's ideology influenced India's Naxalite movement, which synthesized Maoism with Marxism-Leninism, as well as Peru's Shining Path. He presided over the Sino-Soviet split with Nikita Khrushchev, calling him "revisionist" after Khrushchev denounced Stalin in the Secret Speech, and employed the scientist Qian Xuesen — deported from the United States during the Second Red Scare — as the head of China's space program.

comprehensive_summary: Mao Zedong was the founding Chairman of the People's Republic of China, whose founding he declared on October 1, 1949, and whose portrait still hangs over Tiananmen Square; his quotations and aphorisms, collected in the Little Red Book, made him the founder of a namesake variant of Marxism-Leninism. He rose to leadership of the Chinese Communist Party by taking power from Bo Gu and Otto Braun (the "returned student faction") at the Zunyi Conference during the Long March, later narrating his autobiography to journalist Edgar Snow in Yan'an, who published it as Red Star Over China and improved Mao's public image; Mao referred to the atomic bomb as a "paper tiger." He allied with his rival Chiang Kai-shek against Japan following the Xi'an Incident, forming the Second United Front, having developed theories of protracted guerrilla warfare while living in a cave and comparing the guerrilla to "a fish who swims within the sea of the people" in a 1937 book; he also declared China would "lean to one side" and wrote On New Democracy. As a theorist, Mao is best known for the essay "On Contradiction," comparing opposing classes to a bomb — "a single entity in which opposites coexist" — and arguing that contradictions are universal; he originated the phrases "political power grows out of the barrel of a gun" and "women hold up half the sky," divided the world into three categories of nations in his "Three Worlds Theory" (partly in response to Alfred Sauvy), delivered the Talks at the Yan'an Forum on Literature and Art, and praised Norman Bethune, a man he had met only once, for his "spirit of absolute selflessness." He also retold a folk tale of a Foolish Old Man who moved mountains, substituting "the masses of the people" for God, and used the metaphor of a "hero with no place to display his prowess" to argue for raising literary standards. Mao's quotations were assembled into the Little Red Book by his eventual designated successor Lin Biao, who praised Mao as a genius; the book, widely distributed during the Cultural Revolution, includes sections on "Democracy in the Three Main Fields," "Criticism and Self-Criticism," and the "Mass Line" ("the people alone are the motive force in world history"), and its portrait of Mao became the basis for Andy Warhol's silkscreen paintings. In 1956, Mao briefly encouraged citizens to voice their true opinions of the government in the Hundred Flowers Campaign, comparing artistic and scientific flourishing to "a hundred flowers blooming"; the movement was short-lived and followed by a crackdown. His Great Leap Forward included a campaign against the "Four Pests" (such as sparrows) and forced peasants toward metal production, contributing to a famine remembered as the "three years of natural disasters"; the overreporting of crops planted via "deep plowing," tied to his "General Line" concept (one of the Three Red Banners), accompanied accusations of mass cannibalism, and propaganda staged a photo of children standing on a hidden bench atop a thickly planted wheat field. The era's abuses are documented in The Corpse Walker, the pseudonymous Scarlet Memorial by T.P. Sym, and Frank Dikotter's The Tragedy of Liberation; Mao also praised the red pepper as "the food of the true revolutionary," and a dentist was executed under his rule after calling an object "just like a sweet potato and not special." During the Cultural Revolution, Mao gifted Pakistani mangoes to workers after they suppressed student revolts, and the mangoes — preserved in formaldehyde by recipients unwilling to eat them — became cult symbols; he called himself the "black hand" suppressing rival student factions and told dissenters to "Bombard the Headquarters" amid corruption accusations aired on "big-character posters," later titling his own poster the same. He was nearly the target of "Project 571," a coup plot by his designated successor Lin Biao, who fled and died in a plane crash in Mongolia after the plot was uncovered; he also summoned the leaders of a local government formed by the "January Storm" (modeled on the Paris Commune), forced them to normalize their rule, and feuded with the president of the Chinese Academy of Sciences over a reconciliatory poem. He claimed to have swum 15 kilometers in 65 minutes in 1966 — mocked by Judy-Joy Davies as "the greatest swimmer of all time" — having swum across the Yangtze River in both 1956 and 1966. His death in 1976 and the subsequent arrest of the Gang of Four, led by his wife Jiang Qing, ended the Cultural Revolution. Mao's abandonment of his wife He Zizhen for Jiang Qing was criticized in Ding Ling's essay "Thoughts on March 8" and depicted in Anchee Min's novel Becoming Madame Mao; his first wife was active in the Cultural Book Institute and was executed by a warlord after refusing to recant, and He Zizhen, a skilled guerrilla fighter educated at Moscow's East University whose legitimacy as his wife was later denied, was supplanted by Jiang Qing, a former Lu Xun Academy teacher who designed several "model operas," feuded with Liu Shaoqi and Zhou Enlai, and was ultimately removed via a secret meeting orchestrated by Li Xiannian before leading the Gang of Four after Mao's death. Mao remains a fixture of world culture: his name is rhymed with a line in the Beatles' "Revolution 1," he appears as a recurring narrator in Campbell McGrath's poetry collection XX, and Don DeLillo's novel Mao II, titled for an Andy Warhol silkscreen of him, follows a novelist unable to finish his third book; Mao himself wrote the poem "Reply to Li Shuyi." A giant portrait of him unnerves the narrator in Perhat Tursun's Uyghur-language novel The Backstreets, his surname recurs in Madeleine Thien's Do Not Say We Have Nothing and Anchee Min's Becoming Madame Mao, and the Misty Poets rejected the literary classicism he championed. In John Adams's opera Nixon in China, he is visited by Richard Nixon and emerges from a hidden door in a giant self-portrait to sing "I am no one." He has been painted waving from a boat in Strive Forward in Wind and Tides, reimagined visiting Venice by Erro Gudmundur, depicted with blue eyeliner and rouge in a 14-foot portrait at the Art Institute of Chicago, rendered as three iconic jackets in the sculpture Legacy Mantle, and portrayed in a widely reproduced contrapposto image as a young man traveling to Anyuan. Internationally, his ideology influenced India's Naxalite movement and Peru's Shining Path; he presided over the Sino-Soviet split with Nikita Khrushchev, calling him "revisionist," and employed the scientist Qian Xuesen, deported from the United States during the Second Red Scare, as the head of China's space program.
